title: The Magicians of Caprona — Chapter 2 (Fragment 1)
sourceText:
The Magicians of Caprona

Chapter 2

Tonino was already feeling soothed by the bustle in the golden streets of Caprona. In the narrower streets, he walked down the crack of sunlight in the middle, with washing flapping overhead, playing that it was sudden death to tread on the shadows. In fact, he died a number of times before he got as far as the Corso. A crowd of tourists pushed him off the sun once. So did two carts and a carriage. And once, a long, gleaming car came slowly growling along, hooting hard to clear the way.

When he was near the Corso, Tonino heard a tourist say in English, “Oh look! Punch and Judy!” Very smug at being able to understand, Tonino dived and pushed and tunneled until he was at the front of the crowd and able to watch Punch beat Judy to death at the top of his little painted sentry-box. He clapped and cheered, and when someone puffed and panted into the crowd too, and pushed him aside, Tonino was as indignant as the rest. He had quite forgotten he was miserable. “Don’t shove!” he shouted.

“Have a heart!” protested the man. “I must see Mr. Punch cheat the Hangman.”

“Then be quiet!” roared everyone, Tonino included.

“I only said—” began the man. He was a large damp-faced person, with an odd excitable manner.

“Shut up!” shouted everyone.

The man panted and grinned and watched with his mouth open Punch attack the policeman. He might have been the smallest boy there. Tonino looked irritably sideways at him and decided the man was probably an amiable lunatic. He let out such bellows of laughter at the smallest jokes, and he was so oddly dressed. He was wearing a shiny red silk suit with flashing gold buttons and glittering medals. Instead of the usual tie, he had white cloth folded at his neck, held in place by a brooch which winked like a teardrop. There were glistening buckles on his shoes, and golden rosettes at his knees. What with his sweaty face and his white shiny teeth showing as he laughed, the man glistened all over.

Mr. Punch noticed him too. “Oh what a clever fellow!” he crowed, bouncing about on his little wooden shelf. “I see gold buttons. Can it be the Pope?”

“Oh no it isn’t!” bellowed Mr. Glister, highly delighted.

“Can it be the Duke?” cawed Mr. Punch.

“Oh no it isn’t!” roared Mr. Glister, and everyone else.

“Oh yes it is,” crowed Mr. Punch.

While everyone was howling “Oh no it isn’t!” two worried-looking men pushed their way through the people to Mr. Glister.

“Your Grace,” said one, “the Bishop reached the Cathedral half an hour ago.”

“Oh bother!” said Mr. Glister. “Why are you lot always bullying me? Can’t I just—until this ends? I love Punch and Judy.”

The two men looked at him reproachfully.

“Oh—very well,” said Mr. Glister. “You two pay the showman. Give everyone here something.” He turned and went bounding away into the Corso, puffing and panting.

For a moment, Tonino wondered if Mr. Glister was actually the Duke of Caprona. But the two men made no attempt to pay the showman, or anyone else. They simply went trotting demurely after Mr. Glister, as if they were afraid of losing him. From this, Tonino gathered that Mr. Glister was indeed a lunatic—a rich one—and they were humoring him.

“Mean things!” crowed Mr. Punch, and set about tricking the Hangman into being hanged instead of him. Tonino watched until Mr. Punch bowed and retired in triumph into the little painted villa at the back of his stage. Then he turned away, remembering his unhappiness.

He did not feel like going back to the Casa Montana. He did not feel like doing anything particularly. He wandered on, the way he had been going, until he found himself in the Piazza Nuova, up on the hill at the western end of the city. Here he sat gloomily on the parapet, gazing across the River Voltava at the rich villas and the Ducal Palace, and at the long arches of the New Bridge, and wondering if he was going to spend the rest of his life in a fog of stupidity.

words.definition: To make calm or tranquil; to relieve or ease.
words.example: Tonino was already feeling soothed by the bustle in the golden streets of Caprona.
words.exampleText: Tonino was already feeling soothed by the bustle in the golden streets of Caprona.

words.definition: Busy, energetic, and noisy activity.
words.example: By the bustle in the golden streets of Caprona.
words.exampleText: Tonino was already feeling soothed by the bustle in the golden streets of Caprona.

words.definition: Of small width.
words.example: In the narrower streets, he walked down the crack of sunlight in the middle.
words.exampleText: In the narrower streets, he walked down the crack of sunlight in the middle.

words.definition: A main street or promenade in an Italian town.
words.example: He got as far as the Corso.
words.exampleText: He died a number of times before he got as far as the Corso.

words.definition: Shining brightly, especially with reflected light.
words.example: A long, gleaming car came slowly growling along.
words.exampleText: A long, gleaming car came slowly growling along.

words.definition: Making a loud sound to warn or signal, especially with a horn or whistle.
words.example: Hooting hard to clear the way.
words.exampleText: Hooting hard to clear the way.

words.definition: Having or showing an excessive pride in oneself or one's achievements.
words.example: Very smug at being able to understand.
words.exampleText: Very smug at being able to understand, Tonino dived and pushed and tunneled.

words.definition: A small shelter from which a sentry guards.
words.example: At the top of his little painted sentry-box.
words.exampleText: Punch beat Judy to death at the top of his little painted sentry-box.

words.definition: Feeling or showing anger or annoyance at what is perceived as unfair treatment.
words.example: Tonino was as indignant as the rest.
words.exampleText: Tonino was as indignant as the rest.

words.definition: To express an objection to what someone has said or done.
words.example: “Have a heart!” protested the man.
words.exampleText: “Have a heart!” protested the man.

words.definition: Easily excited or stirred emotionally.
words.example: He was a large damp-faced person, with an odd excitable manner.
words.exampleText: He was a large damp-faced person, with an odd excitable manner.

words.definition: Having or displaying a friendly and pleasant manner.
words.example: The man was probably an amiable lunatic.
words.exampleText: Tonino decided the man was probably an amiable lunatic.

words.definition: A mentally ill person (informal) or someone behaving in a wildly foolish or eccentric way.
words.example: He was probably an amiable lunatic.
words.exampleText: Tonino decided the man was probably an amiable lunatic.

words.definition: Shining brightly with flashes of light.
words.example: Glittering medals and flashing gold buttons.
words.exampleText: He was wearing a shiny red silk suit with flashing gold buttons and glittering medals.

words.definition: An ornamental pin worn on clothing.
words.example: Held in place by a brooch which winked like a teardrop.
words.exampleText: He had white cloth at his neck, held by a brooch which winked like a teardrop.

words.definition: A guardhouse or small shelter for a sentry.
words.example: Punch beat Judy at the top of his little painted sentry-box.
words.exampleText: Punch beat Judy at the top of his little painted sentry-box.

words.definition: To shout something with a deep loud roar.
words.example: “Oh no it isn’t!” bellowed Mr. Glister.
words.exampleText: “Oh no it isn’t!” bellowed Mr. Glister.

words.definition: To make a harsh cry, like a crow.
words.example: Can it be the Duke?” cawed Mr. Punch.
words.exampleText: “Can it be the Duke?” cawed Mr. Punch.

words.definition: Causing anxiety or concern.
words.example: Two worried-looking men pushed their way through the people.
words.exampleText: Two worried-looking men pushed their way through the people to Mr. Glister.

words.definition: Expressing disapproval or disappointment.
words.example: The two men looked at him reproachfully.
words.exampleText: The two men looked at him reproachfully.

words.definition: In a reserved, modest, and shy manner.
words.example: They went trotting demurely after Mr. Glister.
words.exampleText: They went trotting demurely after Mr. Glister.

words.definition: To comply with the wishes or moods of someone to keep them content.
words.example: They were humoring him.
words.exampleText: They were humoring him.

words.definition: A great victory or achievement.
words.example: He retired in triumph into the little painted villa.
words.exampleText: He retired in triumph into the little painted villa.

words.definition: In a sad or depressed manner.
words.example: Here he sat gloomily on the parapet, gazing across the river.
words.exampleText: Here he sat gloomily on the parapet, gazing across the River Voltava.

words.definition: A low protective wall along the edge of a roof, bridge, or balcony.
words.example: He sat gloomily on the parapet.
words.exampleText: Here he sat gloomily on the parapet, gazing across the River Voltava.

translations.ru: туман, дымка; в переносном смысле — помутнение ума

words.definition: A thick cloud of tiny water droplets near the earth's surface; figuratively, confusion or lack of clarity.
words.example: Wondering if he was going to spend his life in a fog of stupidity.
words.exampleText: Wondering if he was going to spend the rest of his life in a fog of stupidity.
